text: GATTEO, a village of the Pontifical states, in the leg. and 20 m. ESE of Forli. Fairs are held here twice a-year.

GATTEVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Manche, cant. of Saint-Pierre-Eglise. Pop. 1,308. It gives its name to the promontory called the Raz-de-Gatteville, which forms the E angle of the peninsula of Cotentin, and to the E of the bay of the same name, in N lat. 49° 41' 45", and W long. 3° 36′ 30″, and on which the lighthouse of Barfleur is situated.
GATTICO, a village of Piedmont, in the prov. and 21 m. NNW of Novara. Pop. 1,020.
GATTNAU, a village of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Danube, bail. and 6 m. S of Tettnang, and 72 m. SSW of Ulm. Pop. 1,400.
GATTO, or GAVATA (CAPE), a headland of the island of Cyprus, on the S coast, in the sanjak and 40 m. SE of Baffa, in N lat. 34° 32′ 50″, and E long. 32°.
GATTON, a parish in Surrey, 9 m. SSW of Croydon, and on the Croydon railway. Area 1,140 acres. Pop. 219.
GATTONSIDE, a beautifully situated village, on the I. bank of the Tweed, about 1 m. N of Melrose, Roxburghshire. Pop. 252.

GATUN, a village of New Granada, in the dep. of Ystmo, on the Chagres river, 5 m. above Chagres, and 6 m. below the confluence of the Trinidad with the Chagres. At present it is only a group of some 50 or 60 huts, constructed of branches, and covered with palm-leaves. The railway now in progress across the isthmus runs from Navy bay to G., and then keeps the E bank of the river towards Gorgona.
GATUZIE'RES, a village of France, in the dep. of the Lozere, cant. and 2 m. NE of Meyruis, on the | GAU Jonte. Pop. 282. A cattle fair is held here once a-year.

GAUBE (LAC-DE), a small lake of France, in the dep. of the Hautes-Pyrénées, cant. and 14 m. S of Argeles. It lies on the granite range stretching SW from the valley of Bastan to Panticoso; and discharges itself by a river of the same name. The scenery around is extremely grand and desolate.
GAUBOCKELHEIM, a town of Hesse-Darmstadt, in the prov. of the Rhine, 5 m. W of Wörstedt, on the Apfel. Pop. 1,250.
GAUBRETIE'RE (LA), a village of France, in the dep. of the Vendee, cant. and 7 m. WSW of Mortagne. Pop. 1,628. Iron is wrought in the environs.
GAUBURGE (SAINTE), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Orne, cant. of Merlerault. Pop. 701.

GAUCHOS, a name given to a family of Spanish origin, who are found scattered in various parts of La Plate, from Buenos- Ayres to San Luis and Mendoza, and in the N part of the Pampas. Although sunk to a condition little superior to that of savage life, living in mud huts, and subsisting exclusively on the produce of the chase, they are courteous and hospitable in disposition, and still profess allegiance to the Roman Catholic faith. The chief supplies of cattle, horses, fowls, and eggs, to the markets of Buenos Ayres are furnished by this people.

GAUCIN, or GAUZIN, a judicial partido and town of Andalusia, in the prov. of Malaga. The partido comprises 9 pueblos. The town is 49 m. WSW of Malaga, and 24 m. N of Gibraltar. Pop. 4,815. It occupies a commanding position amid the Ronda mountains, and contains a parish church, 2 convents, and a public granary. It has several distilleries of brandy, and soap manufactories, and 2 tanneries.
GAUDE (LA), a village of France, in the dep. of the Var, cant. and 2 m. E of Vence, near the l. bank of the Caone. Pop. 692. The environs afford good wine.

GAUDENS (SAINT), an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Garonne.—The arrond. contains an area of 210,918 hect., and comprises 11 cant., viz., Aspet, Aurignac, Bagnères-de-Luchon, Saint-Beat, Saint- Bertrand, Boulogne, Saint-Gaudens, L'Isle-en- Dodon, Saint-Martory, Montrejean, and Salies. Pop. in 1831, 139,789; in 1841, 144,116.—The cant. comprises 22 com. Pop. in 1831, 19,511; in 1841, 19,581.—The town is 54 m. SW of Toulouse, on the I. bank of the Garonne, on the road from Bagnèresde-Bigorre to Toulouse. Pop. in 1841, 5,459. It has a college, a custom-house, an agricultural society, and board of trade; and possesses manufactories of china, common cloth, and other woollen fabrics, hats and tape, numerous saw, fulling, paper, wool-spinning, flour, and oil-mills, several glass and tileworks, tanneries, and 2 printing establishments. It has an active trade in grain, and fairs for cattle are held 4 times a-year. This town is noted as the birth-place of Remond, founder of the order of Calatrava in Spain.

GAUDENZIO (SAN), a town of the grand duchy of Tuscany, in the prov. and 33 m. ENE of Florence, vicariat and 21 m. ENE of Pontassieve near the r. bank of the Dicomano. It has a church, a foundling hospital, and possesses some manufactories of woollen fabrics. Pop. 900.
GAUDINIERE (LA), a hamlet of France, in the dep. and on the r. bank of the Sarthe, cant. and 5 m. NW of Frenay-le-Vicomte, com. of Sourgé-le-Galonne. It has several iron-works.
GAUDONVILLE, a town of France, in the dep. of the Gers, cant. and 5 m. E of St. Clar-de-Lomagne. Pop. 500.
GAUGNIET-CHAUSSE'E, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault. Pop. 451.
GAU-EL-GARBIEH, a village of Egypt, in the prov. and 30 m. SE of Siout, on the l. bank of the Nile.
GAU-EL-KEBIR, a town of Egypt, in the prov. and 30 m. SE of Siout, on the r. bank of the Nile. It occupies the site of the ancient Anteopolis, and has the remains of a magnificent temple.
GAUIR, a river of Perthshire, which has its source near Loch Etive, and falls into Loch Rannoch.

GAUJACQ, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Landes, cant. of Amon, 15 m. SW of Saint- Sever, on a small stream, near the confluence of the Luy-de-France and Luy-de-Bearn. Pop. 1,018. It has a saline spring, a bed of coloured gypsum, and a mine of bitumen.
GAULBY, a parish of Leicestershire, 7 m. ESE of Leicester. Area 1,170 acres. Pop. 108.
GAULEY, a river of the state of Virginia, U.S., which has its source on the W side of the Alleghany mountains, and flows into the great Kanawha river, on the N side, a little above the great falls.

GAUKARNA, or GACURNUM, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Canara, 21 m. NW of Onore, and 78 m. SSE of Goa, on the Indian ocean, near the mouth of the Cholmolly, and in the midst of a forest of cocoa-nut palms. It consists of about 500 houses, inhabited chiefly by Brahmins. A considerable trade in salt, the produce of the adjacent salt marshes, is here carried on.
GAULBY, or GALBY, a parish of Leicestershire, 8 m. E of Leicester. Area 1,170 acres. Pop. in railway, 7 m. SE of Baucen. Pop. 345. 1831, 118; in 1841, 108.
GAULEY BRIDGE, a village of Fayette co., in the state of Virginia, U. S., 277 m. W of Richmond, on the Great Kanawha river, which is here 500 yds. in width, and has a perpendicular fall of 22 ft.

GAULNA, or Galna, a district, fort, and town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Candeish. The district, which forms the S part of the prov., is generally mountainous, but is well-watered, and possesses considerable fertility. Its chief towns besides Gaulna are Chandore, Loneir, Nampur, and Wakari. The fettah or town of G., 87 m. NW of Aurungabad, is enclosed on the NE and W by high barren hills, and to the S is a fine valley, bounded by the termination of the Western Ghauts. It is surrounded by a mud wall, flanked with towers; and is defended on the E by a fort which crowns a lofty rocky eminence, enclosed by a strong wall, 20 ft. in height, and about 1 m. in circumf. This place was ceded to the British government at the peace of Mandwir in 1818.
GAULSKILL, or Gallskill, a parish in co. Kilkenny, 41 m. N of Waterford. Area 1,303 acres. Pop. in 1831, 322; in 1841, 350.
GAULT (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of Loir-et-Cher, cant. of Droue, 24 m. NNW of Vendome. Pop. 1,292.

GAULTIER (SAINT), a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Indre, arrond. of Leblanc.— The cant. comprises 8 com. Pop. in 1841, 6,980.— The com., on the r. bank of the Creuse, 18 m. E of Blanc, has a pop. of 1,622.
GAUNA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 9 m. E of Alava. Pop. 1,130.
GAUNERSDORF, a town of the archd. of Austria, in the circle and 15 m. NE of Korneuburg. Pop. 600.
GAU-ODERNHEIM, a town of Hesse-Darmstadt, in the prov. of Rhein-Hessen, on the r. bank of the Selze, 5 m. ENE of Alzey. Pop. 1,514.

GAUR, a ruined city of Hindostan, in the district of Kharwa, in Puraniya, between the Mahananda and the Ganges. In the time of the Hindu government it was a place of great extent; and its ruins GAU was removed to Tangra, and subsequently to Rajmahal. Many of the towns on the Mahananda, and even a great part of Murshedabad, were supplied with bricks from the ruined and deserted city.
GAURAIN-RAMECROIX, a commune and village of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, 4 m. E of Tournay. Pop. 2,717.
GAURIAC, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Gironde, cant. of Bourg-sur-Gironde, 4 m. SSE of Blaye. Pop. 1,774.

GAURITZ, a river of S. Africa, formed by the union of the Gamka or Little Lowen, and the Dwika or Rhinoceros river, both rising on the S flanks of the Nieuwevelde's Berge, but at a distance of nearly 100 m. from each other. The two streams unite in about S lat. 33° 17', E long. 21° 45', on the N side of the Black mountains, and flow S to the Indian ocean, separating the district of George on the E, from those of Worcester and Zwellendam, on the W. The principal tributary of the G. on the l. is the Olifant's river, and on the r. the Buffels and the Tau. The total course of the combined stream is about 130 m., but in direct distance only 75 m. During the summer-months, the G. has but a weak current, and the bar at its mouth is then generally dry; but in the rainy season, it is the most rapid and dangerous river in the colony.
GAUSIG, a village of Saxony, in the circle of Upper Lusatia, near the line of the Saxon-Silesian
GAUSS (CAPE), a low point of land, with a small islet off it, in the Antarctic continent, in S lat. 76° 09', E long. 162° 52'.
GAUSSON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cotes-du-Nord, cant. of Plouguenast, 8 m. N of Loudeac. Pop. 2,323.
GAUSTAFJIELD, or Gousta-fjell, a mountain of Sweden, in the prov. of Christiansand, bail. of Bradsberg, in 59° 40' N lat., W of Tind lake. Alt. 5,608 Swedish or 4,801 English ft.
GAUTBY, a parish of Lincolnshire, 6 m. NW by N of Horncastle. Area 2,010 acres. Pop. in 1831, 109; in 1841, 99.
GAUTEGUIZ-DE-ARTEAGA, a village of Spain, in the prov. and 21 m. ENE of Bilbao. Jasper is quarried in the vicinity.
GAUTRAINS, a village of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. and 5 m. NE of Rignac, near the source of the Alson. Pop. 175.

GAUTS, or GHAUTS, a very extensive and remarkable mountain-system in Peninsular or Southern Hindostan. They are divided into the Eastern and Western G. The latter, which are called Sukhein- Purbut, or 'hills of Sukhein,' stretch from Cape Comorin to the Tupti river near Surat, in a line nearly parallel with the sea-coast; their general distance from it being 40 m.; in some places 70 m.; and in one part only 6 m. At the Tupti their direction is suddenly changed from N, with a little inclination W, to E, running nearly parallel with that river. These G. run through 13 degrees of lat., in an almost unbroken course, except opposite to Paniani, where a valley extends 14 or 15 m. between the termination of the Northern G. and the commencement of the Southern. Through this valley the Paniani flows from the Coimbatur. In that part of the Western G. which runs parallel to the Tupti, there are several passes from which there is a descent into the prov. of Khandeish. There are also some passes from the Concan district of the provs. of Bejapur, the principal of which are the Ambah-G. and the Tourna-G.: for the word gaut, ghaut, or ghát, signifies 'a port,' 'pass,' or 'defile,' through a mountain-range. cover an area of nearly 20 sq. m.; but it seems to The mountains near the Ambah-G. rise to a great have rapidly decayed after the seat of government | height. The pass at Tourna is much more rugged and steep than that of Ambah, and in the middle of | it there is an extensive plain. The principal pass from the Mysore country through the Western G. is Bergeley G., which leads into the maritime prov. of Canara. As this pass is much frequented for the purpose of conveying grain by means of oxen to the sea-coast, and bringing back salt, the road has been formed with great labour; notwithstanding which, the strength and rapidity of the torrents are such as frequently to wash away all the smaller and softer parts, and to leave single rocks 4 or 5 ft. in diam. in the centre of the road, not above 2 ft. asunder. This pass is often travelled at night by torch-light. The Western G., especially about the lat. of 15°, are covered with a rich mould, on which various forest-trees grow with great luxuriance. Their height has not been measured; but it is supposed to be about 5,000 ft. The Eastern G. do not extend so far S as the Western range: beginning only a little N of the Caveri, about the parallel of 11° E. Their line is nearly straight, and is uninterrupted as far as the banks of the Kristna, in lat. 16°, forming a chain of mountain-defiles which separates the table-land of the peninsula from the coast district. At Madras their height is greatest. It is estimated at 3,000 ft.; as Bangalore, in lat. 12° 57', and E long. 77° 46', lying within the G., was ascertained to be 2,901 ft. above Madras. They consist almost exclusively of syenite, with a small proportion of felspar. To the S of the Nerbudda all the rivers flow E. The Eastern G. are not so high as the Western; they are also less abrupt in their elevation; and in their general character are less fertile and pleasing, exhibiting naked, sun-burnt, and barren peaks. To enter the Mysore country there are several passes, the principal of which are those of Mugli, Palicand, Amhar, Changama, Attur, and Sautgur. The last was always considered of great importance, as it commands the main road leading to the Upper Carnatic from the valley of Veniambody in the Barramakahal, which is the most direct route to and from the Mysore. This pass has been levelled and widened since the British gained possession of the prov. The other passes all centre in the Palicand pass. Hence, though Hyder and Tippu possessed the advantage of all these routes, when they attacked the low country, they had but Tijuco. one entrance at this part into the Mysore to defend.
GAUTSCH, a village of Saxony, 4 m. S by W of Leipzig. Pop. 505.
GAUTULCO. See GUATULCO. Santafé.
GAUTUMPUR, or GHATUMPUR, a town of Hindostan, in the Doab, 18 m. E of Calpi.
GAUVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Orne, cant. of La-Ferte-Fresnel. Pop. 1,007. There is a mineral spring in the vicinity.
GAUWELSTRAET-ET-PACHT, a village in the prov. of W. Flanders, cant. of Avelghem. Pop. 425.
GAUZENS (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Tarn, cant. of Graulhet, on the l. bank of the Adou. Pop. 1,220.
GAVARDIN, a village of Turkey, in Bulgaria, on the r. bank of the Vid, 18 m. NNE of Isladi.
GAVARDO, a town of Austrian Lombardy, in the deleg. and 10 m. ENE of Brescia, on the Chiese, which flows through it. Pop. 1,880.

GAVARNIE, a village of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Pyrenees, cant. and 9 m. S of Lus, on a gave of the same name, an affluent of the Gaye-de-Pau, at an alt. of 1,409 metres or 4,620 ft. above sea-level. Within an hour's walk of this place is a magnificent mountain scene called the Cirque or Circus. It has the figure of a horse shoe, and is surrounded by impending precipices and glaciers, alternately disposed one above the other, in a series of gigantic steps, whilst from the highest a circle of torrents falls | GAV in cascades. The arena of the Cirque—which is locally called the Oule, i. e. 'the caldron'-is of prodigious extent, and consists entirely of debris fissured and worn by the torrents that hurry through it. One of these cascades, issuing from a lake on Mont Perdu, leaps down a precipice of 1,200 ft.
GAVATA. See GATTO (CAPE).

GAVAUDUN, a town of France, in the dep. of Lot-et-Garonne, cant. and 6 m. NNE of Monflanquin. Pop. 1,026. *** GAVE, a word synonymous with riviere in the Bearn dialect in the S of France, and the local generic name of numerous streams in the Pyrenees. It is probably derived from the same root as our avon. Sometimes it is applied to a district of country. Thus the dio. of Lescar is called the Gave Bearnais.
GAVE', a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Brabant, dep. of Glimes. Pop. 97.

GAVELLO, a town of Austrian Lombardy, in the prov. of Polesina, district and 4 m. NNE of Crespino, and 9 m. ESE of Rovigo, near the r. bank of the canal Bianco. Pop. 1,960. It occupies the site of a town of the same name which was destroyed by an inundation of the Adige and Po.
GAVENOE, an island of Denmark, in the diocese of Sieland, bail. of Proestöe, in the gulf of Restvader. It is 3 m. in length, and about half that extent in breadth.
GAVER, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of East Flanders, dep. of Calcken. Pop. 93.
GAVETTO, a maritime town of Algiers, in the prov. and 60 m. NE of Constantina, and 48 m. W of Bona, on the Mediterranean.

GAVI, a mandamento and fortified town of Piedmont, in the prov. and 5 m. S of Novi, in a valley on the r. bank of the Lemme, and on the road from Genoa to Alessandria. Pop. of town, 1,600; of mand., 5,702. GAVIA, GABIA, or GAVEA, a group of mountains, in Brazil, in the Tejuco range, in the prov. and 15 m. SE of Rio-de-Janeiro, near the coast, in N lat. 22° 59'. Its highest point, called from its square form 'Topsail mountain,' is well known to English sailors by the name of 'Lord Hood's Nose.' It is granitic, and rises nearly perpendicularly from the sea to the height of about 2,000 ft. The top is flat. At its base are the roadstead and the bar of the Rio GA'VIA - LA - CHICA and GA'VIA - LA -
GRANDE, two pueblos or villages of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. of Granada and partido of

GAVIAO, a mountain of Brazil, in the prov. of Ceará, which runs between the districts of Januarie and Granja.—Also a river in the prov. of Bahia, which waters the district of Villa-do-Principe, receives the Santo Antonio, and unites with the Contas.
GAVIAON, or GARVAO, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Alemtejo, comarca and 21 m. NW of Crato, on a height about 1 m. S of the Tagus. Pop. 1,117.

GAVILAN (SIERRA DE), a range of mountains in the central part of the island of Cuba, extending SE a distance of about 45 m., uniting on the E with the Sierra-de-Molias, and forming a section of the great chain which intersects the island in its entire length. It sends S a ramification which terminates near La Trinidade.
GAVINO (SAN), a village of the island of Sardinia, in the prov. and 29 m. NNW of Cagliari, about 3 m. N of a stream of the same name. Pop. 1,880.

GAVINTON, a modern village in the p. of Langton, Berwickshire, 12 m. from Dunse, and 52 m. from Greenlaw, on the road between these towns. It occupies the site of the ancient village of Langton. Pop. 206.

GAVIRATE, a district and village of Austrian Lombardy, in the gov. and 38 m. NW of Milan, I prov. and 20 m. W of Como, on the N bank of the lake of Varese. Pop. 1,300. It has a weekly market. The district contains 26 com. Pop. 12,982.
GAVIRIA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Guipuzcoa, partido of Azpeitia, 5 m. WSW of Villafranca. Pop. 900. It has two mineral springs.
GAVOR, or JAWUR-DAGH, a mountain of Turkey in Asia, on the confines of the pash. of Marash and Aleppo. It extends about 15 m. in an ESE direction, and contains numerous Armenian villages.

GAVORRANO, a town of Tuscany, in the comp. and 21 m. NW of Grosseto. Pop. 1,600. It has several thermal mineral springs. The preparation of charcoal, bark, potash, and timber from the adjacent forests, forms the chief branch of local industry.

GAVRAY, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Manche, arrond. of Coutances. The cant. comprises 15 com. Pop. in 1841, 15,382.—The town is situated on the Sienne river, 12 m. SSW of Coutances. Pop. 1,838. It has manufactories of linen and parchment, and a large cattle and sheep market.
GAVRE (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of Loire-Inferieure, cant. of Blain, 17 m. NW of Savenay. Pop. 1,436.
GAVRE, a town of Belgium, in the prov. of E. Flanders, cant. of Oosterzell, on the r. bank of the Scheld. Pop. 1,209.—Also a village in the same prov., in the arrond. of Helchin. Pop. 189.
GAVRI, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Wilna, 27 m. WSW of Rossiena.
GAVRIKOVA, a port of Russia, in the gov. of Archangel, in N lat. 69° 5'.
GAVRILOFSK, a village of Asiatic Russia, in the gov. of Tomsk, district of Barnaul, on the Tolmavaia.
GAVRUS, a village of France, in the dep. of Calvados, cant. and 2 m. NNW of Evrecy. Pop. 163. Marble is quarried in the vicinity.

GAWCOTT, a chapelry in Bucks, 2 m. SSW of Buckingham. Area 550 acres. Pop. in 1821, 566; the Nizam's territory, in the NE part of the prov. of Berar; bounded on the N and E by Gundwana; on the S by Mahore; and on the W by Nernallah. It is traversed by the Purnah. Its chief towns are Ellichpur and Amravatti.—It derives its name from a fortress, one of the strongest in S. India, situated on a range of mountains between the sources of the Purna and the Tapti, to the S of the village of Labada, and about 20 m. NW of Elichpur. It stands on a lofty rock; and consists of a complete inner fort fronting the S, where the rock is most steep, and an outward fort which covers the inner to the NW and N. The outer fort has a third wall covering the approach to it from the N. This fastness, though defended by a brave and skilful commander, with a garrison of 5,000 hardy Rajputs and Ghoseins, was stormed and carried by the British troops under Major-general Wellesley, in December 1803, within eight and forty hours from the opening of their batteries. There are some fine remains within the walls; but all are now overrun with rank weeds of gigantic growth and jungle grass. The hills in the environs have for ages past been a favourite retreat for that extraordinary sect the Jhains, a group of whose temples are situated upon the precipitous banks of a mountain-torrent at a place called Muhtagheni, a little to the NW of G.

GAYA, a river of Spain, which rises in the prov. GAY of Lerida, 15 m. SE of Cervera; and, entering Tarragon, flows into the Mediterranean near Tamarit, 6 m. E of Tarragon, after a course of 36 m. from N to S.—Also a town of Moravia, in the circle and 18 m. W of Hradisch, on the Stupawka. Pop. in 1834, 1,922, of whom 502 were Jews.—Also an island in the Sulu archipelago, near the E coast of Borneo, in N lat. 4° 46', E long. 118° 53'. It is about 6 m. in circumf., and has a good port.—Also a town of Hin. dostan, in the prov. of Bahar, 60 m. SSW of Putna, on the l. bank of the Fulgo or Phalgu, in N lat. 24° 49'. It consists of two parts, G. Proper, which is the residence of the Brahmins, and Sahib-ganj, i.e., 'Gentleman's town.' The latter is the modern part of the city, and has wide streets. Between the two towns is an open space, called the Rumnah, on which the courthouses stand. The old town has narrow, crooked, dirty, and uneven streets, and presents singular architecture in its houses, the greater part of which are of brick and stone, ornamented with projecting corners, turrets, and galleries. When the Mahrattas invaded the district, they were on all occasions repulsed by the pop. of G. under the leadership of the priests. The Buddhists regard this place as having been the birth-place of their great legislator; it is consequently a celebrated place of pilgrimage. The number of pilgrims is said to be increasing, and in some seasons to fall little short of 200,000. The permanent pop. of the town is about 40,000. An immense number of ruined temples appear in the vicinity of this place.
GAYBIEL, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 40 m. NNW of Valentia. Pop. 1,400.
GAYDON, a parish in Warwickshire, 3 m. NE of Kington. Area 1,140 acres. Pop. in 1831, 213; in 1841, 276.
GAYHEAD, a village in Greene co., in the state of New York, U. S., 39 m. SW of Albany.
GAY HEAD, the western section of the island of Martha's Vineyard, in Duke co., in the state of Massachusetts, U.S. There is a light-house on its W extremity, with a revolving light elevated 150 ft.
GAYLES, a township in the p. of Kirkby-Ravensworth, N. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. NW by N of Richmond. Area 1,840 acres. Pop. in 1831, 223; in 1841, 186.
GAYSING, or GAJAR, a town of Hungary, in the com. and 24 m. NNW of Presburg. It has an active trade in cattle.
GAYSTEAD, a parish in Northumberland, in the NW division of Tyndale ward. Area 25,980 acres. Pop. 219.

GAYTON, a township in the p. of Heswall, in Cheshire, 3 m. NW by N of Great-Neston. Area 1,840 acres. Pop. in 1831, 223; in 1841, 149.—Also a p. in Norfolk, 7 m. E by S of King's Lynn. Area 2,990 acres. Pop. in 1831, 711; in 1841, 789.—Also a parish in Northamptonshire, 4 m. N of Towcester. Area 1,580 acres. Pop. in 1831, 461; in 1841, 428. -Also a p. in Staffordshire, 5 m. NE of Stafford. Area 1,270 acres. Pop. in 1831, 296; in 1841, 291.
GAYTON-LE-MARSH, a parish in Lincolnshire, 6 m. NNW of Alford. Area 2,250 acres. Pop. in 1831, 306; in 1841, 312.
GAYTON-LE-WOLD, a parish in Lincolnshire, 6 m. W by S of Louth. Area 1,730 acres. Pop. in 1831, 127; in 1841, 125.
GAYTON-THORPE, a parish in Norfolk, 8 m. NW by N of Swaffham. Area 2,110 acres. Pop. in 1831, 169; in 1841, 179.
GAYWOOD, a parish in Norfolk, 1 m. E of | King's Lynn. Area 2,380 acres. Pop. in 1831, 924; in 1841, 1,064.

GAZA, a town of Syria, 48 m. SW of Jerusalem, in N lat. 31° 29', 3 m. from the coast of the Mediterranean. It is a considerable town, advantageously situated upon an eminence about 2 m. in circumf. at the base, surrounded with tamarisk, fig, and olive gardens; and is supposed to contain from 3,000 to 4,000 inhabitants, who are a mixture of Arabs and Turks. The streets are narrow. The aga's or governor's mansion is considerable; and the mehkeme or tribunal is a very large edifice. The largest mosque is an ancient Greek church. All the principal edifices are built of a coarse white marble with which the surrounding country abounds. The markets are well-supplied, and water is plentiful and good. The ancient town occupied a site nearer the sea.
GAZACOTTA, or Guzgotta, a town of Bengal, in the district and 8 m. N of Rungpur, in N lat. 25° 50', E long. 89° 15'.
GAZAUPOUY, a town of France, in the dep. of Gers, cant. and 4 m. NW of Condom, near the r. bank of the Lauvignon. Pop. 1,043.
GAZEL. See GASAL.
GAZELEY, a parish of Suffolk, 4 m. S of Newmarket. Area 5,470 acres. Pop. in 1831, 737; in 1841, 860.
GAZER, a town of Central Africa, in the kingdom of Asben, 60 m. N of Assudi. G., 760.
GAZIMUR, a river of Asiatic Russia, in the gov. of Irkutsk, in the district of Nerchinsk, which flows into the Argun, on the l. bank, after a course of 210 m. from SW to NE.
GAZIPUR. See GHAZIPORE.
GAZIR, a village of Syria, in the pash. of Acre, 36 m. W of Balbek, near the Mediterranean.
GAZTELUGACHE, an island of Spain, in N lat. 43° 29', SW of Cape Machichaco, on the coast of Biscay.
GAZUOLO, or GAZOLDO, a town of Austrian Lombardy, in the prov. and 12 m. WNW of Mantua. Pop. of district, 2,000.
GAZZANIGA, a town of Austrian Lombardy, in the prov. and 9 m. NE of Bergamo, on the r. bank of the Serio. Pop. 1,350.
GAZZO, a town of Austrian Lombardy, in the prov. and 9 m. NNE of Vicenza. Pop. 1,600.

GDOV, a town of Russia, in the gov. and 126 m. SW of St. Petersburg, on the E bank of Lake Peipus, in N lat. 58° 35'. Pop. 1,000.—Also a village of Austrian Galicia, in the circle and 15 m. WSW of Bochnia, on the Rassa.
GEA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 15 m. W of Teruel, on the l. bank of the Guadalaviar. Pop. 1,200.
GE'ANT (AIGUILLE DU), a summit of the Pennine Alps, to the W of Mont Blanc, of an estimated alt. of 13,099 ft. above sea-level.

GE'ANT (Col-DU), a pass in the Pennine Alps, bearing NNE, 2 m. from the Aiguille du G., in N lat. 45° 49' 54", E long. 6° 49' 6". It has an alt. of 11,146 ft. above sea-level, or 9,803 ft. above Geneva, and has been repeatedly ascended. De Saussure and his party arrived upon it on the 3d July, 1788, and remained 17 days at this great elevation. Professor Forbes, who ascended the Col from the valley of Courmayeur, and Mont Frety, and descended its N side to Chamouni in one day, has described the magnificence of the wide Alpine view from this point in his Travels through the Alps.

GEASHILL, a parish and village in King's co., comprising the vs. of Ballinagar and Killeigh. Area 43,309 acres. Pop. in 1831, 10,267; in 1841, 10,077. The v. is 31 m. S by W of Philipstown. Pop. in GEE 1841, 327.—The family of Digby take the title of baron from this p.
GEAUGA, a county in the NE of Ohio, U.S., watered by the head-branches of the Cuyahoga and Chagrin rivers. Area 600 sq. m. Pop. 16,297. The cap. is Chardon.

GEAUNE, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Landes, arrond. of Saint-Sever.—The cant., comprising 19 coms., had a pop. of 9,278 in 1841.—The com. is situated on the r. bank of the Bas, 12 m. SE of Saint-Sever. Pop. in 1841, 1,045. It has some commerce in cattle and wool.
GEBA. See JEBA.
GEBEL. See JEBEL.
GEBERGTE, a village of Belgium, in the prov. of Antwerp, arrond. of Meerhout. Pop. 165.
GEBESEE, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Saxony, circle and 9 m. SE of Weissensee, on the Gera. Pop. 1,845. It was greatly devastated by fire in 1750.
GEBHARDSDORF, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Silesia, circle and 12 m. SSE of Lauban. Pop. 1,640. It has manufactories of linen and turnery ware.
GEBHARTS, a village in Somerset co., in the state of Pennsylvania, 148 m. W of Harrisburg.
GEBRA (Lower and Upper), two villages of Prussian Saxony, in the prov. of Erfurt, 9 m. WSW of Nordhausen. Pop. of Upper G., 493; of Lower
GEBRAZHOFEN, a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Danube, bail. and 4 m. SW of Leutkirch. Pop. 356, Catholics.

GEBY, an island in the Molucca archipelago, in the strait of Gilolo, in S lat. 0° 5', E long. 129° 30'. It is 23 m. long, and 3 m. broad; and is composed of two mountains connected by a low slip of land. The soil is fertile in sago, spices, and fruits. The inhabitants are Papuas and Malays. It has a good harbour on its SW side, in E long. 129° 38', S lat. 0° 6'.
GECHI-BURLU, a small town and kadilik of Anatolia, in the sanj. of Hamid, 9 m. S of Burlu.
GEDAN. See KIDAN.
GEDDINGTON, a parish in Northamptonshire, 5½ m. E by N of Bothwell. Area 2,140 acres. Pop. in 1831, 795; in 1841, 833. Henry II. convened a parliament here in 1118.
GEDEM, a town of Hesse-Darmstadt, in the prov. of Upper Hesse, 9 m. NE of Ortenberg, on an affluent of the Nidder. Pop. 1,862.
GEDINGUMA. See ELIMANE.
GEDINNE, a village of Belgium, in the prov. and 45 m. S of Namur on the Houille. Pop. 444.
GEDLING, a parish in Nottinghamshire, 31 m. N by E of Nottingham. Area 4,490 acres. Pop. in 1831, 2,343; in 1841, 2,642.
GEDNEY, a parish in Lincolnshire, 3 m. E by S of Holbeach. Area 12,110 acres. Pop. in 1831, 1,862; in 1841, 2,277; of whom 480 were in the chapelry of Gedney-hill.
GEDUMA, a state of W. Africa, on the N frontiers of Senegambia, on the E side of the Senegal, which separates it from the Futatoro territory, and having Ludamar on the E.
GEEL. See GHEEL.
GEELEGEM, a village of Belgium, in the prov. of E. Flanders, arrond. of Alost. Pop. 123.

GEELONG, a town and township of South Australia, in the co. of Grant, on the W side of Port Phillip. The town is situated 8 m. from the sea, on the N or l. bank of the Barwon, and near the head of the W branch of Port Phillip, called G. harbour, 40 m. SW of Melbourne, with which it has steamcommunication. It is a rising town, and already conducts a large trade in grain and wool. The ~ country to the W, between G. and Lake Colac, is a fine open district, well-adapted either for cattle or tillage, and lightly timbered.

GEELVINK BAY, or GREAT BAY, a large bay on the N coast of New Guinea, on the N of the isthmus by which the SE part of that island is united to the NW section, between the parallels of 0° 45' and 4° 15' S lat., and the meridians of 134° 30' and 138° E. It is nearly 200 m. wide at its entrance; and penetrating nearly 200 m. S into the body of the island, at its upper extremity reaches a point only 18 m. from the Molucca sea. Its navigation is dangerous from the number of small islands and rocks which are scattered about in it.

GEER, or JAAR, a river of Belgium, in the prov. of Limburg, rising in Liege, in two streams which unite near Lens-sur-Geer. The united stream flows NE to Tongres, and then sweeps round in an E, SE, and NNE course, and falls into the Meuse on the 1. bank, under the walls of Maestricht.

GEERTRUIDENBERG, a strongly fortified town of Holland, in the prov. of N. Brabant, arrond. and 9 m. NNE of Breda, and 19 m. W of Bois-le-Duc, in N lat. 51° 42' 3", on the Biesboch. It has a small port, and conducts an active fishing-trade. Pop. 1,625.

GEESH, or GEISH (MOUNT), a mountain of Abyssinia, in about 11° N lat., and 37° E long., to the S of Lake Zana or Dembea. Alt. 9.700 ft. It is connected with the Talba-Waha mountains; and the Abai has its rise in a swampy tract at its foot, at about 2,600 ft. above the level of Lake Dembea. The marsh is full of springs, and thickly overgrown with kosso trees, and a yellow-flowering shrub called amija. On the opposite side of the plain of Bahrzafa, to the W of Mount G., rises Mount Lichema, which attains a considerably greater alt.
GEESTE, a river of Hanover, rising to the E of Bremervorde, and flowing WNW to the estuary of the Weser, into which it flows at Bremerhafen, after a course of about 30 m.
GEESTENDORF, a village of Hanover, in the landr. of Stade, 13 m. SSE of Bremerhafen. Pop. 532.
GEESTEREN, a village of Holland, in the prov. of Gelderland, 5 m. S by E of Lochem.
GEFALL, a town of the archd. of Austria, in the circle of Manhartsberg, 9 m. NNW of Krems.
GEFELL, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Saxony, circle of Ziegenruck, 57 m. SE of Erfurt. Pop. 1,482. Iron, and kaolin or porcelain clay, are mined in the vicinity.
GEFFEIN, a village of Holland, in the prov. of North Brabant, 8 m. NE of Bois-le-Duc. Pop. 975.
GEFFOSSES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Manche, cant. of Lessay, 9 m. NW of Coutances. Pop. 1,298.

GEFLE, a town of Sweden, the cap. of the laen or prov. of Gefleborg, situated near the embouchure of a small river of the same name, about 1 m. from the gulf of Bothnia, in N lat. 60° 39' 45", E long. 17° 8' 30", 50 m. N by W of Upsala. It is divided into 4 quarters by the river, which, flowing from the Stornjon lake to the SW, forms two islands above its entrance into the gulf. The town is well-built, and the streets are wide and well-paved; but a large proportion of the houses are of wood, and painted white. The chief buildings are the church, the governor's house, the town-hall, built by Gustavus III., and an hospital. The town is the seat of a gymnasium, 2 schools, and an orphan-asylum; and has a fine court-house, and a public library. Pop. in 1833, 8,086. Its harbour is one of the best on the gulf. Large ships lie close to its quay; and a considerable trade is carried on from it in exporting timber, pitch, tar, potash, and iron; and importing GEH grain, salt, and flax. In 1839, 82 vessels <math>= 11,760</math> tons entered this port; and 101 = 16,338 tons cleared outwards. In 1849, 84 vessels entered, of which 10 were from England; and 116 cleared for foreign ports, of which 40 were to British ports. Ship-building is carried on here to a considerable extent; and sailcloth, coarse linens, and leather, are manufactured. Steam-communication is maintained with Stockholm.—G. is the Gevalia of the Latin descriptions of Sweden. The name is frequently pronounced by the natives as if it were written Yaveley.
